coredata.dc:title: A novel cathodic electrochemiluminescent sensor based on CuS/carbon quantum dots/g-C3N4 nanosheets and boron nitride quantum dots for the sensitive detection of organophosphate pesticide
coredata.dc:creator: Kamyabi, Mohammad Ali
Moharramnezhad, Mohsen
coredata.dc:description: In this project, a novel promising electrochemiluminescence probe based on ternary nanocomposite as CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS was demonstrated for the accurate monitoring of diazinon as a popular organophosphate pesticide. This nanocomposite as a promoted luminophore was synthesized by a simple microwave method. Carbon quantum dots and copper sulfide nanostructures displayed a good cooperative effect on the signal improvement. Also, boron nitride quantum dots were introduced as a new and effective co-reactant in the negative potential window for a carbon-based electrochemiluminescence system for the first time. The ultra-sensitive electrochemiluminescence platform was truly used for the analysis of diazinon. The broad linear dynamic range and low limit of detection were obtained from 1.0 × 10−15 to 5.0 × 10−9 M and 2.2 × 10−16 M, respectively. Owing to superior proficiency in accurate diazinon analysis, the developed sensor was used as a new applicable probe for quantitative analysis of diazinon in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Ternary nanocomposite
Boron nitride quantum dots
Diazinon

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of bulk g-C3N4 and g-C3N4NS
The preparation protocol of ternary nanocomposite modified electrode
FT-IR study of the ternary nanocomposite
Voltammetry behavior of ferrocyanide on the different modified electrodes
EIS investigation of the modified electrode
Study the surface morphology of the modified electrode
The ECL behavior of the modified nanocomposite electrode
Evaluation of the optimal conditions on ECL response
Analytical usage of the developed sensor
Sustainability investigation of the fabricated sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the University of Zanjan Research Council for their support of this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Nyquist plots of various GC electrodes in 0.1 M NaOH including 1.0 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− with the AC amplitude of 10 mV and frequency range between 100 kHz and 100 mHz, (B) XRD pattern of CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS ternary nanocomposite, FESEM pictures of (C) g-C3N4NS/GCE, and (D) CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS/GCE at high enlargement scale, (E, F) TEM images of CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS nanocomposite.
EDX spectrum of modified (A) CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS/GCE, EDX mapping analysis (B-F) modified CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS/GCE, (B) C, (C) N, (D) Cu, (E) S, and (F) combine.

(A) ECL-potential, and (B) CV curves of (a) bare GC, (b) CQDs/GCE, (c) g-C3N4NS/GCE, (d) CuS/CQDs/GCE, (e) CuS/g-C3N4NS/GCE, (f) CQDs/g-C3N4NS/GCE, and (g) CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS/GCE in 0.1 M NaOH solution including 1 mg/mL BNQDs at the scan rate of 50 mV/s. (B) ECL-potential curves of the modified nanocomposite electrode in (a) the absence, and (b) the presence of DA in 0.1 M NaOH solution including 1 mg/mL BNQDs at the scan rate of 50 mV/s, [DA]: 6.0 × 10−12 M.
Effects of (A) BNQDs, (B) NaOH, (C) scan rate, and (D) potential region on the optical response of the modified nanocomposite electrode, [DA]: 6.0 × 10−12 M.

(A) ECL-time and (B) calibration graph for the fabricated sensor in different concentrations of DA (a-g) in 0.1 M NaOH solution including 1.0 mg/mL BNQDs at the scan rate of 50 mV/s, (C) 20 consecutive optical signals of the fabricated sensor, (D) reproducibility for eight fabricated electrodes, (E) long-time stability of the ECL sensor, and (F) anti-interfering performance of the developed sensor (a) background, (b) DA, (c) DA + 1.5 × 10−9 fenpropathrin, acetamiprid, and thiamethoxam, (d) DA + 1.8 × 10−9 M deltamethrin, cypermethrin, and fenvalerate, (e) DA + 7.2 × 10−10 M p-nitrophenol, o-nitrophenol, 2,4-dichlorophenol, and 2,4,6-trichlorophenol, (f) DA + 6.0 × 10−9 M K+, Na+, and NH4 +, (g) DA + 3.0 × 10−9 M SO4 2−, Cl− and NO3 –, (h) DA + 6.0 × 10−10 M Ni2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ in 0.1 M NaOH solution including 1.0 mg/mL BNQDs at the scan rate of 50 mV/s, [DA]: 6.0 × 10−12 M.
The fabrication protocol of GC electrode with ternary nanocomposite for ECL monitoring of DA.
Comparison of various sensors for analysis of DA.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
(1)
LDH: layered double hydroxides, ZIF: zeolitic imidazole framework, (2)
CL: chemiluminescence, (3)
AChE: acetylcholinesterase, UCNPs: up conversion nanoparticles, (4)
FL: fluorescence, (5)
MIP: molecularly imprinted polymer, CP: carbon paste electrode, (6)
SWV: square wave voltammetry, (7)
DPV: differential pulse voltammetry, (8)
QDs: quantum dots, GNP: graphene nanoplatelets, CMWCNTs: carboxylated multi wall carbon nanotubes, DZBA: diazinon binding aptamer, BSA: bovine Serum Albumin, (9)
RuNPs: ruthenium nanoparticles, GO: graphene oxide.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diazinon analysis of the various real samples in comparison with GC–MS method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
(1)
: Detected by mean of 3 times of analysis.

head.ce:title: A novel cathodic electrochemiluminescent sensor based on CuS/carbon quantum dots/g-C3N4 nanosheets and boron nitride quantum dots for the sensitive detection of organophosphate pesticide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Zanjan, Postal Code 45371-38791, Zanjan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Zanjan, Postal Code 45371-38791, Zanjan, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this project, a novel promising electrochemiluminescence probe based on ternary nanocomposite as CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS was demonstrated for the accurate monitoring of diazinon as a popular organophosphate pesticide. This nanocomposite as a promoted luminophore was synthesized by a simple microwave method. Carbon quantum dots and copper sulfide nanostructures displayed a good cooperative effect on the signal improvement. Also, boron nitride quantum dots were introduced as a new and effective co-reactant in the negative potential window for a carbon-based electrochemiluminescence system for the first time. The ultra-sensitive electrochemiluminescence platform was truly used for the analysis of diazinon. The broad linear dynamic range and low limit of detection were obtained from 1.0 × 10−15 to 5.0 × 10−9 M and 2.2 × 10−16 M, respectively. Owing to superior proficiency in accurate diazinon analysis, the developed sensor was used as a new applicable probe for quantitative analysis of diazinon in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: The uncontrolled increase in the use of pesticides and chemical fertilizers in order to improve the amount of horticultural and agricultural products has increased the more risk the contamination of food products and the environment [1]. Therefore, the control of pesticides at low concentrations is a vital issue and has attracted the attention of many scientists. Organophosphate pesticides (OPS) with high poisoning effects are very harmful to humans and ecosystems even at low concentrations. Therefore, plenty of efforts with high sensitivity and admissible accuracy have been investigated in the field of OPs monitoring [2]. Diazinon (DA), as a famous Ops family, is still widely applied to control the variety of pests in horticultural crops. Also, DA with good soluble in water, and its stability has a high penetration power into groundwaters. Therefore, many water sources can be contaminated with this pesticide. Most sensitive tools such as chromatographic [3], colorimetric [4], photoluminescence [5,6], and electrochemical probe [7] for the accurate analysis of diazinon were investigated. However, chromatographic-based techniques are expensive, and they need a long-time to complete the test, and the use of enzymatic methods is limited due to some enzyme defects such as instability in temperature, humidity, changes of pH, and the high price of the enzyme. In addition, electrochemical techniques do not present lower detection limits. Therefore, providing a quick, stable, and sensitive tool is necessary for the accurate detection of diazinon in different samples [8]. Electrochemiluminescence (ECL), with its remarkable properties, has become a robust analytical tool that benefits from electrochemical and luminescence methods, in which the generated excited species under the redox reaction produce emissive light. Because of low-expense instrumentation, excellent versatility, near-zero background, and good sensitivity for most compounds, it can be applied in various aspects such as glucose [9], and pesticide [10,11] sensors.

Quantum dots (QDs) are a new group of nanoparticles whose size varies from 1 to 100 nm and have unique properties such as higher optical stability, narrow excitation, and emission spectrum. Boron nitride (BN) in nanoscale exhibits interesting properties such as high thermal conductivity, large bandgap (5.7 eV), superior mechanical specification, high chemical stability, low toxicity, etc. Boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) as zero-dimensional nanomaterial belongs to the boron nitride family, which are synthesized from bulk h-BN via the exfoliation process [12]. Due to their supreme optical characterizations, they were utilized as an efficient coreactant for the analysis of biomolecules in the ECL system [13]. Also, one of the most important QDs are carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are very popular owing to their cheapness, availability, good water solubility, and ease of synthesis. Owing to strong fluorescence emission, excellent electrocatalytic, physicochemical, and optical properties, their applications have expanded in most sensors [14].

In the discussion of nanomaterials, nanocomposites have a special position. Carbon-based nanocomposites have received special attention from researchers due to their excellent properties, accessibility, and low cost. To enhance the electron transferin electrochemical methods, significant developments in the use of three-dimensional porous materials have been extensively increased. Graphitized carbon nitride (g-C3N4) with porous-like structure is formed of carbon and nitrogen elements, which is synthesized by thermal polymerization of electron-rich materials such as melamine, urea, thiourea, and cyanamide [15]. This compound is rich in electrons compared to most carbonaceous materials, and it is an organic and non-metallic semiconductor polymer due to its remarkable electronic structure. Owing to unique electronic properties, superior photocatalytic proficiency, low toxicity, low cost, and high stability, this material has been applied in various aspects such as fluorescent [16], and ECL sensors [17,18].

Among inorganic materials, copper sulfide (CuS) or covellite with a 1.21 eV bandgap is a well-known semiconductor, has exhibited remarkable attention in various aspects, including solar cells, batteries, energy storage, photodetectors, photocatalysis, gas sensors, and electrochemical sensors owing to simple preparation, low cost and supreme electrochemical and electrocatalytic proficiency [19]. In the field of ECL biosensors, CuS acts as a co-reaction accelerator with the generation of more radical species to amplify the luminophore signal [20,21]. Also, copper Sulfide semiconductor has been used as ECL sensor due to the property of ECL light-emitting in the negative potential region [22]. The CuS nanostructures have been synthesized using various techniques such as hydrothermal assay, electrodeposition, and sonochemical methods. Nevertheless, saving time, rapid synthesis, and removal of non-aqueous solvents can be significant advantages of the microwave procedure [23].

In this project, we demonstrated a novel enzyme-free ECL platform based on new luminophore materials as CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS nanocomposite in the cathodic potential region in the presence of BNQDs as a novel coreactant for the negative potential window. The prepared nanocomposite was well attached on the surface of the glassy carbon electrode by helping chitosan. The g-C3N4 nanosheets (g-C3N4NS) were selected as a good choice for supporting CQDs, and copper sulfide nanostructures. The present nanocomposite was prepared with a simple one-step microwave tool, which showed a higher ECL signal intensity than any of its components such as CQDs, and g-C3N4NS. The copper sulfide nanostructures were truly formed on the cavity of the g-C3N4NS. In other words, novel ternary CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS nanocomposites were constructed with the aim of providing enhanced electrochemiluminescence activity. The ECL signal of the luminophore was significantly improved in the presence suitable amount of BNQDs. The optical signal of the modified electrode was remarkably amplified in the presence of diazinon. Therefore, the developed CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS/GCE was applied for ultra-trace determination of DA in different real samples with acceptable output.

Diazinon (O,O-Diethyl O-[4-methyl-6-(propan-2-yl)pyrimidin-2-yl] phosphorothioate) was provided from Sigma-Aldrich and utilized in analytical grade. Citric acid, copper (II) acetate, melamine, ethylene glycol, sodium hydroxide, boric acid, dimethylformamide (DMF), ammonia solution (25%), and ethanol (95–96 %) were provided from Merck. Furthermore, urea, and thiourea were purchased from Fluka Company.

Electrochemical and electrochemiluminescence experiments were done via three-electrode systems based on glassy carbon with a diameter of 3 mm (geometric surface area of 0.07 cm2) as a working, platinum wire as a counter, and Ag/AgCl (sat.KCl) as a reference electrode. The ECL cell was prepared with a homemade dish, which was equipped with a quartz window. The optical signals were recorded with HP 1046A Fluorescence Detector Analyzer (the PMT voltage was fixed at 900 V) with Agilent Chem Station software, which was conjugated with Autolab, PGSTAT 101 (Switzerland) with Nova software. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was characterized by PHILIPS EM208S, 100 kV. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) were captured by TESCAN/MIRA3 (Czech Republic). Also, UV–Vis absorption and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were detected by Analytik Jena, specord 210 plus spectrophotometer, and thermo scientific, NICOLET-IS10 spectrophotometer, respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was accomplished with BRUKER D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Germany) equipped with Kα radiation of Cu. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out via Electrochemical Workstation ZIVE SP1 with ZMAN software. To validate the obtained outcomes with the proposed method, gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) was performed with Agilent Company (America) with GC 7890B and Mass 5977A.

BNQDs were synthesized with the prior report with some minor changes [12]. In Brief, 0.4 g boric acid was dissolved in 20 mL of pure DMF. Then, 0.8 mL ammonia solution (25%) was added dropwise, and the solution was deoxygenated for 10 min, by blowing N2. To exfoliate the prepared solution, it was vigorously sonicated with a sonication probe for 15 min, and then was placed in a Teflon autoclave. Finally, hydrothermal condition as 130˚C and 12 h was utilized for the formation of BNQDs. After cooling the autoclave at ambient temperature, the prepared suspension was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 0.5 h to eliminate large harsh particles, and then, it was passed through the 0.22 μm membrane filter, and supernatant including BNQDs was separated. To collect the obtained sediment, the solvent was evaporated with a rotary tool. The synthesized quantum dots were kept at 4° C when not in use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of bulk g-C3N4 and g-C3N4NS
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of the bulk g-C3N4 was carried out with previous reports [24]. Briefly, 6 g melamine was put in the crucible and heated in two consecutive temperature programs at 500 °C, and 520 °C for 4 h. For the formation of the g-C3N4NS, 1 g of the bulk g-C3N4 powder was outspread in Petri dishes, and then, it was annealed in an air vented furnace at 520 °C for 2 h [25]. After this time, a colorless yellow powder indicates that g-C3N4NS were truly obtained.

According to a previous report, a simple microwave path was used to prepare the CQD [26]. For the synthesis of CQDs, 3 g citric acid, and 3 g urea were dissolved in 10 mL water to obtain a bright solution. The solution was irradiated in a microwave oven (750 W) for 5 min. After cooling, the solution changed from a colorless liquid to dark-brown sediment. The precipitate was dark brown and dried in an oven at 60° C for 1 h to remove the volatile species. The powder was then added to 180 mL of water and centrifuged for 10 min to remove insoluble species. The obtained brown CQDs solution was used for the synthesis of CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation protocol of ternary nanocomposite modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The ternary nanocomposite based on CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS was synthesized according to the following method. Briefly, 0.02 g g-C3N4NS was ultrasonically dispersed in 20 mL deionized water for 5 min.Then, 200 µL CQDs (2.0 mg/mL in deionized water) was added to the solution and the resulting light yellow solution was stirred for 15 min. In the following, 0.01 g copper (II) acetate as a metal foundation, and 1 mL ethylene glycol as a reducing agent were added to the prepared solution. Afterward, 0.005 g thiourea was dissolved in 1 mL deionized water and was added dropwise to the main solution for 1 h in the stirring condition. The obtained solution was put in the microwave oven, and was irradiated for 3 min under the power of 850 W. Finally, the prepared green suspension was washed three times with water and ethanol and dried at 60 °C overnight. For the modification of the glassy carbon electrode with ternary nanocomposite, the GC electrode was polished with alumina powder. Then, it was sonicated in acetone and deionized water to gain a fresh surface and was dried for 5 min at 50˚C in an oven. 1.0 mg of the prepared nanocomposite was well dispersed in 1.0 mL deionized water, and 10 μL of 1.0 mg/mL above suspension was put on the surface of the fresh glassy carbon electrode, which was dried at ambient temperature for 1 h. Finally, 5.0 μL of the 0.6% chitosan in 1% acetic acid solution was put on the modified electrode surface, and was dried at 4˚C for 2 h. The modification process and ECL behavior of the fabricated glassy carbon with ternary nanocomposite are represented in scheme 1 .

For the analysis of diazinon, electrochemical, and electrochemiluminescence tests were accomplished with a three-electrode probe in which a fabricated CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS/GC electrode as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode, and silver/silver chloride (sat.KCl) as the reference electrode. Also, 0.1 M NaOH solution was used as the electrolyte medium in this work. The ECL tests were performed via cyclic voltammetry method in the negative potential region from 0.0 V to −2.0 V at the scan rate 50 mV.s−1 and the output signals were recorded with a fluorescence detector analyzer equipped with a photomultiplier tube (PMT-900 V).

TEM image was performed to depict BNQDs, which was presented in Fig S1(A). The average size of BNQDs was obtained less than 10 nm exhibits that these QDs were truly synthesized. Fig S1(B) shows the XRD pattern of the BNQDs. As clear from this figure, the weak diffraction peak at 2θ value of 26.6° (JCPDS: 01–085-1068) was significantly broadened. These results confirm that the crystallization of the boron nitride was decreased during the strong exfoliation process [27]. From the UV–Vis spectrum in Fig S1(C), there is one absorption peak of BNQDs, which is emerged below 200 nm [28]. Finally, FT-IR spectra were also used to evaluate the functional groups on the BNQDs. As from Fig S1(D), the broad peak near 3370 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching mode of the O–H and N–H groups [29,30]. The peaks at ≈1100, 1633, and 2800 cm−1 are in accordance with N–B–O, C–(BN), and –CH3 vibrations, respectively, which approve the presence of the solvent molecules, and oxygenated species on the surface of BNQDs [27]. The obtained findings from this section confirm that there are plentiful active functional groups at the surface of synthesized QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR study of the ternary nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: To clarification the functional group in the structure of CQDs, g-C3N4NS, and synthesized nanocomposite, FT-IR spectroscopy was utilized in the region between 400 and 4000 cm−1, which was represented in Fig.S2 of the supplementary file. For CQDs, the wide peak above 3000 cm−1 approves the presence of –OH stretching. Also, the peaks in 1722, 1590, and 1110 cm−1are in accordance with C=O, –NH2, and C–O, respectively. Furthermore, the weak peak at 1765 cm−1 was observed to a small amount of carboxylic acid on the CQDs surface. In the FT-IR spectrum of g-C3N4NS, the wide peak above 3000 cm−1 is ascribed to O–H and N–H stretching modes [31]. Various peaks were observed in the region of 1200–1700 cm−1 approves the distinguished stretching vibrations of aromatic C–N, and C=N. The sharp peak at 809 cm−1 is in accordance with the heptazine derivatives [32]. In the spectrum of the CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS, the peak at 618 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching modes of Cu–S [33]. Also, a weak peak at 2370 cm−1 can be ascribed in g-C3N4NS, and prepared nanocomposite, which indicates the presence of the atmospheric CO2 [34]. These findings confirm that no remarkable change was observed for the C–N ring structure of graphitic carbon nitride in the nanocomposite by exfoliation and microwave route for the deposition of CQDs and CuS nanostructures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Voltammetry behavior of ferrocyanide on the different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The cyclic voltammetric behavior of ferrocyanide was performed on the surface of different modified electrodes in 0.1 M NaOH solution to demonstrate the electron transfer rate..The CV studies showed the reversible behavior of [Fe(CN)6]4− on the bare and the modified glassy carbon electrodes, which can be attributed to the fast electron transfer kinetics at the electrode surfaces. As shown in Fig.S3, the redox currents increased after modification of the GCE with g-C3N4NS, and CQDs/g-C3N4NS, respectively. For the modified ternary nanocomposite electrode, maximum current was observed due to the excellent electrocatalytic properties of CuS [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS investigation of the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) of the different modified electrodes was carried out in alkaline solution including 1.0 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4−, which is indicated in Fig. 1 A. The high frequencies in the semicircular region, and the low frequencies in linear region illustrate charge transfer resistance (Rct), and mass transfer resistance (Rmt), respectively. Obviously, after the surface of GC was covered with g-C3N4NS, the Rct decreased. Also, by adding CQDs on the modified g-C3N4NS/GC, the Rct shifted to the lower amounts. Finally, after the modification of the glassy carbon electrode with synthesized ternary nanocomposite, the Rct decreased remarkably due to the presence of high conductive copper sulfide nanostructures [36,37]. So, from EIS outcomes, it can be concluded that the fabricated electrode provides a good charge transfer kinetic in the ECL process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study the surface morphology of the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1B displays the X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum of CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS, which was evaluated to approve the structure of the prepared nanocomposite. As clearly seen from this figure, different sharp peaks were located at 2θ values 27.6˚, 29.1˚, 31.7˚, 32.7˚, 47.9˚, 52.6˚ and 59.1˚ that correspond to the covellite hexagonal form of copper sulfide nanostructures (JCPDS:01–078-0876). On the other hand, broadening of the background signal in 2 θ values between 20˚ and 35˚ indicates the presence of carbonaceous materials such as CQDs, and g-C3N4NS. The peak at 2 θ of 29.1˚, which overlaps with the copper sulfide peak in this region, is also related to g-C3N4NS (JCPDS: 01–078-1747). These findings approve that the ternary nanocomposite was truly synthesized.

The FESEM images were utilized to represent the surface morphology of the different electrodes, which are depicted in Fig. 2 (C, D). As clear from Fig. 2C, the graphitic carbon nitride in sheet form is well attached on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode. Fig. 2D represents the ternary nanocomposite modified electrode. In this figure, the deposited CuS nanostructures over graphitic carbon nitride are obviously observed.

Transmission electron microscopy was used to better representation of the synthesized nanocomposite. Fig S4 shows the TEM images of CQDs, and CQDs/g-C3N4NS, which confirms that the CQDs were synthesized. As shown in Fig S4 (B), the prepared CQDs were well dispersed among the g-C3N4NS. Furthermore, TEM image in Fig. 2E shows over a thin layer of g-C3N4NS, and CQDs well loaded on the cavities of g-C3N4NS. Also, Fig. 2F exhibits the presence of CuS nanostructures in the ternary nanocomposite. These findings approve that the CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS was truly synthesized via microwave-assisted.

EDX analysis is a useful tool for elemental clarification. Fig. 3 A confirms the presence of carbon, nitrogen, copper, and sulfur on the modified glassy carbon electrode. In addition, EDX mapping was investigated to confirm the elemental broadcast of different species on the fabricated electrode surface. Fig. 3(B-F) displays the uniform deposition of carbon (violet), nitrogen (yellow), copper (red), and sulfur (green). These results confirmed that the ternary nanocomposite based on CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS was well distributed on the electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ECL behavior of the modified nanocomposite electrode
ce:section.ce:para: To consider the proficiency of the modified nanocomposite electrode, it was necessary to evaluate the ECL behavior of CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS/GCE in the alkaline solution. As shown in Fig. 3A, in the presence of BNQDs as a strong co-reactant in the solution, the bare glassy carbon electrode has no optical response (curve a).

For the modified CQDs/GCE, as the ECL luminophore and BNQDs as the coreactant, the ECL reactions were carried out according to the reactions (1)–(4), (Fig. 3A, curve b). The CQDs capture electrons in the negative potential region and become CQDs−• [38,39]. Next, the ECL signal is generated by the interaction of BNQDs−• species with CQDs−• species,
(1) CQDs + e− CQDs−•.
(2) BNQDs + e− BNQDs−•.
(3) CQDs−• + BNQDs−• CQDs*.
(4) CQDs* CQDs + hʋ.

For the modified g-C3N4NS/GCE, the optical response was observed in the negative potential scan. At the potential of less than −1.4 V vs. Ag/AgCl, the g-C3N4 is reduced to g-C3N4 −• by taking electron [40]. Also, BNQDs are converted to BNQDs−• by reducing in the negative potential region [27]. In the following, by the interaction of BNQDs−• species with g-C3N4 −•, g-C3N4* species are generated that produce ECL signal. The proposed mechanism for ECL can be summarized by the following reactions (Fig. 3A, curve c) (Eqs, 5–8):
(5) g-C3N4 + e− g-C3N4 −•.
(6) BNQDs + e− BNQDs−•.
(7) g-C3N4 −• + BNQDs−• g-C3N4*.
(8) g-C3N4* g-C3N4 + hʋ.

Also, for CuS-based fabricated electrodes as CuS/CQDs/GCE, and CuS/g-C3N4NS/GCE, according to the reactions of 9, 10, CuS with superior conductivity, and electrocatalytic effects as a co-reaction accelerator reacts with BNQDs to generate more BNQDs−• and the ECL signal of the luminophore is significantly intensified (Fig. 3A, curves d, e) [20].
(9) Cu2+ + e− Cu+.
(10) Cu+ + BNQDs Cu2+ + BNQDs−• (more).

In addition, the ECL signal of the luminophore in the presence of CQDs (CQDs/g-C3N4NS/GCE) was improved due to the superior luminescent effect of these quantum dots by producing radical species as CQDs−• (curve f). Thesespecies increase the population of the radical species and the ECL signal of g-C3N4 is dramaticallyintensified [14]. Eventually, for the modified ternary nanocomposite electrode in the form of CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS/GCE (curve g), the intensity of the optical signal increased more than five fold compared to g-C3N4NS/GCE. This could be due to increase in excited species in the presence of CQDs by producing the radical species of CQDs−• and CuS as a superior co-reaction accelerator increases the population of BNQDs−• in the ECL process [20,41]. Since the electrochemiluminescence depends entirely on the applied potential, the cyclic voltammetry behavior of the different electrodes in the negative potential region was investigated. As can be seen from Fig. 3B, for the bare GCE no reduction peak was observed in the negative potential below −1.4 V. However, for the other modified electrodes, the highest peak current is related to the modified CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS/GCE due to the good electrocatalytic effect of CuS nanostructures. These findings confirm that the CV results are in accordance with the ECL Output.

Furthermore, from Fig. 3C, diazinon, as a popular organophosphorus pesticide is quickly hydrolyzed to diethylphosphorothioate (DEP) at negative potentials [42] and then, the DEP interacts with OOH – as the reactive oxygen species (ROS) to generate DEP–OH [43]. In the following, DEP–OH loses its proton in the alkaline solution and becomes DEP–O−. Probably, DEP–O− interacts with radical species, which are generated from CQDs, CuS, g-C3N4, and BNQDs to increase the population of these radicals [44]. So, the ECL intensity of the fabricated nanocomposite electrode increased by adding diazinon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the optimal conditions on ECL response
ce:section.ce:para: It is necessary to optimize the affected parameters on the luminophore signal intensity. One of the main factors that affect the optical proficiency of the modified electrode is the BNQDs concentration as a selected coreactant. The concentration of coreactant plays an important role in this system. From Fig. 4 A, by adding the concentration of BNQDs up to 1.0 mg/mL, the luminophore optical signal is increased owing to the increase in the amount of BNQDs−• species. At the higher values than 1.0 mg/mL, no obvious improvement in signal intensity was observed, and the optical signals achieved the fixed value. So, this concentration was chosen in this work.

Sodium hydroxide concentration was the other vital parameter on the optical response of the luminophore. As clearly seen from Fig. 4B, due to the better generation of radical species, the ECL peaks also amplified by increasing the electrolyte concentration up to 0.1 M. At higher values than this amount, the intensity of the signals tends to decline owing to the scavenging effect of OH− [45] and 0.1 M was chosen as the supporting electrolyte concentration.

The effect of scan rate on the response of CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS/GC electrode was exhibited in Fig. 4C. When the scan rate was increased up to 50 mV/s, the ECL signals were amplified due to amplifying the excited state in a short time. But, at values above 50 mV/s, the generated radicals do not have adequate time to reach the electrode surface, so the signals are quenched and this value was used in the present study.

Finally, the effect of the potential range was evaluated. As obvious from Fig. 4D, the better optical response of the nanocomposite electrode is attributed to the potential range between 0.0 V and −2.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl. In this applied region, more reactive oxygen species (ROS) are likely to be produced as an optical enhancing agent during the potential scanning [43,46]. These species increase the population of radicals and significantly amplify the Luminophore ECL signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical usage of the developed sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The presented pesticide sensor (CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS/GCE) was applied for the monitoring of DA in the alkaline solution. From Fig. 5 A, by adding DA, the luminophore’s signals gradually increased, which approves the amplifying effect of DA. Fig. 5B displays the calibration graph for the proposed sensor with a linear correlation between ΔIECL and the logarithm of diazinon. For this end, a broad linear dynamic range (LDR), and ultralow limit of detection (LOD) (S/N = 3) were obtained from 1.0 × 10−15 M to 5.0 × 10−9 M, and 2.2 × 10−16 M, respectively. Owing to the cooperative effect of the CuS nanostructures and CQDs in the construction of the novel ternary nanocomposite to amplify the optical signal of the luminophore and also the efficient cathodic co-reactant, the proposed sensor has a tremendous sensitivity for DA compared with other techniques in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sustainability investigation of the fabricated sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5C depicts the 20 continuous ECL signals of CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS/GC electrode that was done by CV in an alkaline solution. To this end, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was gained 1.3 % (n = 20) approves that the fabricated ECL sensor displays superior repeatability. Also, Fig. 5D exhibits the ECL signal of the identical eight modified glassy carbon electrodes with synthesized ternary nanocomposite to evaluate the reproducibility of the fabricated ECL sensor toward a suitable concentration of DA and RSD was calculated 2.4%. Furthermore, the long-time stability of the fabricated sensor has been evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5E, for the proper concentration of DA, the sensor lost only 8.4 % and 17.8 % of the initial response after 15 and 30 days, respectively. Due to these beneficial outputs, the presented sensor can be utilized as a commodious utilization.

It is essential to consider the selectivity of the proposed method in the sensor fields. For this purpose, the effect of some interfering materials was evaluated during the analysis of DA. It was considered that species causing a relative error of less than 5% are not intended as interference in this project. As from Fig. 5F, it was found that most of the interfering species had no notable effect on DA response, which affirms that the presented sensor has notable selectivity for diazinon in the presence of the foreign interfering matrix. In fact, by limiting the potential in the more negative region, the effect of most interfering species on the optical response of the sensor towards DA is negligible.

To consider the utilization of the developed sensor, various water, and fruit samples (Zanjan-Iran) were selected to analyze with this method. Tap water was collected from the city and used without any treatment. The river water was passed through the filter paper to remove impurities and the fruit samples were treated according to the prior literature. The fruit samples used for the real sample analysis were apples and peach, which were obtained from a local supermarket. The samples were washed with deionized water. Then, they were crushed and homogenized by a mixer. 2.0 g of each homogenized sample was placed in tubes, and after adding 4 mL of deionized water, the tubes containing fruit samples were placed in a water bath at 45 ◦C for 20 min. Eventually, the samples were centrifuged for 10 min at 4000 rpm. The clear solution of each tube was collected, and they were diluted (1:3) with deionized water to reach the appropriate concentration. For the proposed method, the recovery values were gained from 99% to 101%. According to Table 2 , the output amounts from the proposed assay were lower than Pesticide Residue Limits (PRL), which was reported by EU about 0.1 μg/L (2.0 × 10−10 M) for any pesticide residues [47]. On the other hand, to validate the present method, the analysis of DA was carried out with GC–MS as a powerful technique. The results in Table 2 display that the acceptable outputs were observed, which confirms the prominent performance of the present procedure. These outputs show that the fabricated sensor could be applied as a novel promising platform for sensitive monitoring of DA in real samples without any pretreatment, and purification steps.

In this work, a novel nanocomposite based on CuS/CQDs/g-C3N4NS/GC electrode was demonstrated for ECL monitoring of diazinon with satisfactory outcomes. The glassy carbon surface was truly coated with nanocomposite using simple dropwise methods. The ternary nanocomposite as a promising luminophore was provided via a single-step microwave route, and then it was used for the construction of the ECL sensor. The presented sensor was very simple and cost-effective compared to enzymatic sensors and expensive chromatographic tools. BNQDs were utilized as a new coreactant for the synthesized ECL luminophore in the negative potential range for the first time. Copper sulfide nanostructures, and CQDs in the body of nanocomposite acted as a superior signal enhancing agent compared to g-C3N4NS only. The results confirmed that diazinon in negative potential has a remarkable amplifying effect on the optical signal of the modified nanocomposite electrode. For the pesticide sensor, a broad LDR (1.0 × 10−15 M − 5.0 × 10−9 M), low LOD (2.2 × 10−−16 M), remarkable repeatability, good selectivity, prominent reproducibility, and long-time stability were gained, which truly implemented as a novel, and innovative electrochemiluminescence pathway for the accurate analysis of diazinon.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the University of Zanjan Research Council for their support of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107421.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
